name: Decarbonising AI With Event-based Neural Networks
description:
Can an artificial neural network be energy efficient?

The artificial neural networks underlying modern AI are very computing intensive, taking a lot of energy to run. In contrast, the human brain has a power budget of only about 20 Watts. In this lecture, Thomas Nowotny from the University of Sussex will explore how spiking neural networks can offer a path in addressing the energy issues that face modern AI systems. Learn about the results of his research in the field deploying trained networks on the Intel Loihi 2 neuromorphic system. Including reflections on interpreting interactions with AI systems.

name: Fat, Sugar, Salt: The Inner Working of Cells
description:
Do cells distinguish between the nutrients they get? What do they do to process different nutrients? What is metabolism at a molecular level, and how does the cell's metabolism determine its fate? 

In this talk, we'll explore the inner workings of cells and how they process different nutrients. We'll explore how 'energy' is made or stored, and why different nutrients result in very different outcomes for cells. The talk will end with how cells are now being used to solve the 'supply' problems for many foods - from milk proteins, to other nutrients.

name: The Governed Gut: Food, Policy, and the Body Politic
description:
Every time you eat, you are at the end of a very long chain of decisions, most of them made by the state.

The walkthrough traces several decades of policy written onto Indian bodies: colonial famines engineered by grain export decisions, forests closed off to foragers by law, the calorie arriving as a scientific standard and becoming a measure of need, the Green Revolution’s bargain, the ration card’s promise. Behind each of these is the same idea that the nation is a body that the state must govern, and your hunger is its business. 

From botanical gardens designed for imperial extraction to algorithms managing delivery workers, join our Experimentor Sai as we piece together how mechanisms of control keep shifting form, but not intent.

name: Plates and Projections: Building Identity, One Bite at a Time
description: Have you ever wondered how every person's fridge is so distinctly different from yours, how we build our identity through our grocery lists or why the olive theory actually works? Join Mediators Tisya and Ashrita on this walkthrough as they unpack how our simple choices act as reflections of identity and personality.

name: Forbidden Desire: Sugar Blowing Workshop
description:
Sugar is a material full of contrasts, a sweet delight and a forbidden indulgence. Inspired by artist Jade Armstrong’s original concept, this workshop explores the delicate balance between beauty, sweetness, and the fleeting nature of life.

In this workshop, Chef Mithuran Chakravarthi will guide the participants to learn and to create intricate sugar-blown sculptures, each capturing a single breath and symbolising both the passage of time and the fragility of life. As these sculptures slowly melt into syrupy residue, they reflect sugar’s transformation from a prized luxury to an overconsumed treat.

This hands-on experience encourages reflection on desire, decay, and consumption.

name: Food as Methodology
description:
What happens when food is used as a method, not just a medium? Can we draw artistic, social, and political insight from food?

Join artist Barney Pau for a deep dive into his exhibit ‘Fortify,’ a film on the fortification of flour that evokes the horror of contemporary food production practices. He will introduce his art practice and how he uses food as both a source of inspiration and a medium. He will explore how queer theory and ecology interact and shape his work, and how he uses the processes of foraging and fermentation as artistic methodologies to unpack complex social, political, and environmental matter.

name: From the Ocean to Us
description:
Is there, truly, plenty of fish in the sea? 

Drawing on visual research on industrial fishing practices, this panel will unpack how mass-scale extraction continues to deplete fish stocks and undermine marine biodiversity, despite growing public awareness and ocean protection initiatives. The work also addresses the often-invisible presence of microplastics within marine ecosystems and human diets, foregrounding the entanglement of environmental harm and everyday consumption.

Join visual artist Martina O'Brien for a discussion of her research, process, and practice in relation to her exhibit ‘Daggertooth’, a moving-image work that critically examines fish consumption within contemporary global food systems. Along with marine experts, she will reflect on the ecological, ethical, and policy dimensions of overfishing and pollution, considering how scientific knowledge, cultural production, and public engagement can intersect. The conversation will create space for dialogue on responsibility, governance, and the future of ocean conservation amid accelerating environmental change.

name: Wetland Walk
description:
Can your food waste give rise to food again? 

Join us for a wetland walk at Rachenahalli Lake as we trace the journey of how food becomes waste. Moving through the city’s sewage and water systems, this session explores the often invisible relationship between urban life and wetlands, and how the two continue to shape one another. Rachenahalli Lake, an urban man-made wetland, filters inflow from the upstream sewage treatment plant at Jakkur Lake. Interestingly, it is also the site of informal fishing practices, where species such as Rohu, Catla, and Mackerel are caught and supplied to local eateries. In this landscape, the walk opens up questions around how wastewater becomes part of ecological and food systems once again.
